Concrete Experience - At this stage
column2: the learner shows personal involvement with others in everyday situations. In concrete situations
column3: learner tends to depend more on feelings
column6: rather than on a systematic approach to situations and problems.

column1: Reflective Observation - At this stage
column2: learners understand situations and ideas from different points of view. They depend on objectivity
column3: patience
column4: and careful judgment but do not essentially take any action. The learners create an opinion on basis of their feelings and thoughts.

column1: Abstract Conceptualisation - At this stage
column2: learners use ideas
column3: logical approaches
column5: rather than interpersonal issues or feelings
column6: to understand situations or problems. In most cases
column7: they depend on systematic planning and building ideas and theories to solve practical issues and problems.

column1: Active Experimentation - At an active stage
column2: the learners show an active learning experience by experimenting with different situations. At the active experimentation stage
column3: the learners take a practical approach
column4: rather than simply observing a situation.

1a)The whole process is governed by six principles
Learning is best conceived of as an ongoing process

column1: 1b)Ideas are continually  transformed through experience

### 1. Concrete experience: Immediacy and felt engagement
*   **Focus:** Assesses whether the reflection is grounded in a specific
column2: lived experience and conveys direct
column3: felt engagement (apprehension).

column1: *   **4 (Strong):** Anchored in a specific experience with genuine immersion
column2: sensory detail
column3: and affective response. Reads as something lived through.

column1: *   **3 (Secure):** Definite experience described with some felt quality and specificity
column2: but immediacy is only partly developed.

column1: *   **2 (Emerging):** Experience is identifiable but reads as a factual summary lacking feeling and specific details.
*   **1 (Limited):** Vague
column2: generalized
column3: or abstract. No concrete

column1: <text>Last Tuesday
column2: I taught  our B1 class a lesson on the present perfect. We focused on using it to tell a friend about places they had visited. We use weak TBLT and during the input phase
column3: students were slower than normal in responding and often gave very short disengaged responses. During the task phase I found students silent in their breakout rooms. Cameras were off
column5: causing disruption. In the subsequent feedback session
column6: I presented my onscreen whiteboard and asked students to annotate
column7: but the response times had lengthened even further so as to make the activity unworkable. </text>"

column1: 3	Evaluating reflective writing			AC	"### 2. Reflective observation: Deliberate
column2: multiperspectival re-examination

column1: *   **Focus:** Assesses whether the writer steps back to patiently re-examine the experience
column2: feelings
column3: and outcomes from more than one viewpoint.

column1: *   **4 (Strong):** Genuine reflective distance. Multiple perspectives (others' viewpoints
column2: competing interpretations) are actively explored to deepen understanding.

column1: *   **3 (Secure):** Clear reflective distance. Acknowledges more than one perspective
column2: though exploration isn't fully sustained.

column1: *   **2 (Emerging):** Reflection remains close to immediate reaction. Other perspectives might be mentioned but aren't genuinely explored.
*   **1 (Limited):** Little/no reflective observation; jumps straight to conclusions or remains locked in a single viewpoint.
<text> The struggle for engagement left me feeling quite distressed.
I recall at the time
column2: being highly self-critical
column3: examining task design
column5: ICQs and feedback mechanisms. I observed that all these elements were adversely affected by learner disengagement. In particular
column8: the erratic pacing and unclear task parameters resulted in a negative feedback cycle.

column1: During the lesson itself
column2: the main locus of attention was on pedagogical quality so I leaned into my training
column3: ensuring my ICQs and CCQs were focused and incisive; staging activities carefully; managing cognitive load by simplifying activities and scaffolding others. Sadly
column4: these interventions didn't make an appreciable difference.

column1: The exit survey surprised me however. I received detailed
column2: engaged feedback
column3: which opened up a route of inquiry I hadn't pursued. They were all saying much the same thing- the teacher needs to choose topics that are relevant and useful. During the lesson
column4: one student had intimated that they didn't understand the topic. One of the most common responses was a slightly dismissive 'I don't know' and a few times
column5: questions were met with an unmuted mic and sigh.

column1: This all left a mark and I recall feeling deeply discouraged. The feedback was unilaterally and concertedly negative and
column2: connecting this to previous experiences
column3: there was an emergent pattern - I encountered the same reaction during a lesson on TOWIE
column4: the students responded in much the same way and were
column6: more outspoken about the irrelevance and utility of the topic. I had spoken to a colleague about this
column7: and she mentioned that the same thing had happened to her when she started using an Oxford coursebook. The students rejected it out of hand she said and she promptly switched back to dogme style lessons

column1: 3	Evaluating reflective writing			AC	"### 3. Abstract conceptualisation: Conceptual transformation
*   **Focus:** Assesses whether the experience is transformed into a principle
column2: concept
column3: or explanatory understanding using logic and symbolic representation.

column1: *   **4 (Strong):** Derives a clear explanatory insight using theory
column2: research
column3: or pattern recognition. Builds a new explanatory account or revises prior understanding.

column1: *   **3 (Secure):** Plausible concept developed
column2: but it is somewhat narrow
column3: under-elaborated
column4: or applied rather than constructed from the experience.

column1: *   **2 (Emerging):** Generic or obvious lesson stated with weak connection to the experience. Little genuine conceptual work.
*   **1 (Limited):** No meaningful conceptualization. Fails to move from observation to explanatory understanding.

<text> Receiving the same feedback and reaction from two classes indicates that the topic focus should be the focus of my attention. Utility may indicate a lack of real world applicability and relevance may indicate materials that are outside learner experience. While both factors are important
column2: I believe that the latter may be most pertinent here. As a western socially privileged teacher
column3: I drew on what I knew and failed to situate the lesson in my learners’ lifeworlds.

column1: A lesson requiring them to talk about holidays and travels in a country devastated by natural disasters and armed violence was not context-sensitive. I was asking them to relate experiences they had little to no familiarity with - experiences which they aspired to have but which may have been unattainable. The lifestyles the lesson conjured up were ones of WASPish luxury and ease. Some activities for instance talked about 5 star hotels and luxury spa retreats. None of my students have travelled outside Haiti.
I would hypothesise that for students living under conditions of exceptional precarity marked by extremes of structural and personal violence
column2: this may have brought home their lack of mobility and the constraints imposed upon them by the  ongoing societal collapse
column3: producing affective barriers to learning. This is supported when i triangulate evidence from my colleague and my previous lesson. In all three cases
column4: the lesson content itself provoked a direct and marked reaction. I recognise that there are other possibilities
column6: perceived pedagogical value or even teaching methodology - students may prefer dialogic

column1: 3	Evaluating reflective writing			AC	"### 4. Active experimentation: Hypothesis-testing action
*   **Focus:** Assesses whether conceptual learning is turned into purposeful future action that acts as a testable hypothesis.
*   **4 (Strong):** Proposes a specific
column2: context-sensitive action linked to conceptual insight. Framed as a test: what they will do
column3: expected outcomes
column4: and how they will measure success.

column1: *   **3 (Secure):** Reasonable future action proposed with links to the conceptual phase
column2: but its testable/experimental quality is only partly developed.

column1: *   **2 (Emerging):** Vague intention given without a clear course of action or testable framework.
*   **1 (Limited):** No meaningful experimentation
column2: or the action is entirely disconnected from the reflection.

column1: <text> My task  for my next lesson then is to plan a class grounded in a Haitian cultural tradition
column2: which draws on the lived experiences of the students in my class. I can draw on our needs analyses to inform the lesson content and also to help me to prepare alternatives and contingency materials. I will assess the efficacy of this intervention through exit surveys adapted to focus on materials and pre-lesson and then interpret the results post lesson. I will also run the materials by my colleagues. I will also continue to observe other factors to ensure that my diagnosis of the situation is correct. Peer observation will be useful here.

column1: To inform my planning process I will send out questionnaires to get an initial sense of what my students value and conduct  focus groups to get an even richer picture of what matters for them. This will be an ongoing process ad I will  use the insights gleaned from the process  to rethink materials design and classroom policy
column2: shaping learning around my students and their needs. </text>

column1: 3	Evaluating reflective writing			AC	### 5. Integrity of the experiential cycle
*   **Focus:** Assesses whether the reflection enacts a genuine
column2: coherent
column3: and cumulative transformation through all four phases
column4: rather than just filling out a template.

column1: *   **4 (Strong):** Coherent
column2: cumulative movement. Observation grows from experience; conceptualization from observation; experimentation from conceptualization.

column1: *   **3 (Secure):** Most transitions are clear
column2: but one link is weaker or formulaic.

column1: *   **2 (Emerging):** Phases are identifiable but loosely connected; reads as separate sections.
*   **1 (Limited):** Fragmented
column2: confused
column3: or mechanically listed without genuine connection.
